meta.title: Latest query loses to stale search results
meta.summary: Users type quickly, but an older slow response overwrites the newest results because requests are not sequenced or cancelled.
signals: Older response lands after newer query
Debounce reduced calls but did not fix stale UI

context.symptom: A user types 'react', then quickly deletes to 're'. The UI briefly shows the latest query, then older results overwrite it.
context.userImpact: Search feels unreliable because the interface does not reflect the most recent intent.
context.environment: Vanilla JS fetch flow with a small debounce, no request tracking, and a shared results store.

evidence.title: Network timeline
evidence.body: Request 17 for 'react' starts before request 18 for 're', but request 17 resolves last and still commits.

evidence.title: Current response handling
Observed behavior
evidence.body: Adding a 250ms debounce reduced traffic but stale results still appear on throttled 3G.

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.prompt: What is the main failure mode?
stages.helperText: Pick the explanation that matches the stale overwrite itself, not just request count.

options.label: Older responses are allowed to commit after newer queries because there is no abort or latest-response guard.
options.feedback: Correct. The problem is not just too many requests; it is that outdated ones can still win the commit.

options.label: The only problem is missing debounce on the search input.
options.feedback: Debounce helps traffic, but stale overwrites can still happen when multiple requests remain in flight.

options.label: JSON parsing is so slow that results arrive in the wrong order.
options.feedback: JSON parse cost is not the signal here. The requests are finishing out of order and committing blindly.

options.label: The browser should use Promise.race so the fastest response always wins.
options.feedback: Fastest is not the same as latest. This framing confuses a race primitive with product intent.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: Rank the three most useful debug steps first.
stages.helperText: You are trying to prove the stale overwrite path quickly and cheaply.

candidates.label: Reproduce on slow network while logging query text and request ids together.
Confirm that an older response resolves after a newer one and still commits.
Inspect whether the code aborts prior requests or ignores stale results.
Rewrite the search endpoint to return smaller payloads first.
Increase debounce to 600ms before you understand the race.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which changes belong in the first robust fix?
stages.helperText: Choose the options that align commits with the latest user intent.

options.label: Abort the previous in-flight request when a newer query starts.
options.feedback: Correct. Abort reduces wasted work and prevents many stale completions.

options.label: Track a request id or query version and ignore responses that are no longer current.
options.feedback: Correct. This protects you even if abort is not honored all the way down.

options.label: Commit results only if the response still matches the current query state.
options.feedback: Correct. Guarding commit time is part of a durable take-latest strategy.

options.label: Wrap all requests in Promise.race so the fastest response updates the UI.
options.feedback: Fastest response is explicitly the wrong goal here. Latest intent is what matters.

options.label: Keep only the debounce; it already reduces overlap enough.
options.feedback: Debounce can help load, but alone it does not guarantee correct ordering.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the strongest guardrail for this class of bug?
stages.helperText: Think about something that proves stale responses cannot overwrite fresh intent anymore.

options.label: Add an integration test that forces out-of-order responses and asserts only the latest query can commit.
options.feedback: Correct. That directly covers the failure mode and prevents a false sense of safety from debounce alone.

options.label: Reduce API latency budgets so requests are less likely to overlap.
options.feedback: Useful, but lower latency does not replace correctness guarantees in the client.

options.label: Do a quick manual smoke test before release and call it done.
options.feedback: Manual checks are helpful, but weak against timing-sensitive regressions.

options.label: Cache every result forever so the client no longer needs to issue new searches.
options.feedback: That changes product behavior and still does not solve stale commit logic.

scoreBands.label: Race condition not yet contained
scoreBands.summary: You treated overlap as a traffic problem instead of a correctness problem.

scoreBands.label: Mostly correct, missing one safeguard
scoreBands.summary: You saw the race, but the debug order or regression guard did not fully lock it down.

scoreBands.label: Take-latest thinking
scoreBands.summary: You proved the stale overwrite, added layered guards, and chose a test that mirrors the failure mode.

idealRunbook: Reproduce with throttled timing and explicit request ids.
Show that older work can commit after newer intent.
Add both abort and latest-response guards.
Protect the flow with an out-of-order integration test.

teachingBlocks.title: Key distinction
teachingBlocks.text: Debounce manages request volume. It does not guarantee correct ordering when multiple responses are still possible.

teachingBlocks.title: Interview framing
items: Name the bug as an async race condition.
Explain why latest intent must win, not fastest response.
Describe both prevention and a regression test.

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: If abort were unavailable in one browser target, what fallback guard would you keep?
